content.text:
Analyze @specs/lex-vietnamese-telex-input-method.md review the tone "reset rules" in the contex of the whole specification to find any gaps, contradictions...

Don't change any content. Incomplete example is acceptable.

Discuss with me to improve the specification.

content.thinking: I need to look at the spec file and understand how the "reset rules" fit into the broader context of the specification.

result.content:
1: # Lex - Vietnamese Telex input method
2: 
3: The specification of the Lex program, a Vietnamese Telex input method.
4: 
5: ## Vietnamese Telex Rules
6: 
7: ### Diacritics
8: 
9: Diacritics let a character has many variant. We only focus on character diacritics in this section.
10: Tones have seperated section.
11: 
12: #### Empty
13: 
14: - Any literal alphabet character has empty diacritic by default.
15: - Only a specific number of characters have other diacritic and it must follow the rules for those
16:   characters.
17: 
18: #### Circumflex
19: 
20: - Valid for: `A` (Â), `E` (Ê), `O` (Ô) and their lower cases.
21: 
22: #### Breve
23: 
24: - Valid for: `A` (Ă) and its lower case.
25: 
26: #### Horn
27: 
28: - Valid for: `O` (Ơ), `U` (Ư) and their lower cases.
29: 
30: #### Stroke
31: 
32: - Valid for: `D` (Đ) and its lower case.
33: 
34: #### Apply rules
35: 
36: - Only apply diacritic when the immediate previous character doesn't have any diacritic (empty).
37: - _Circumflex_, _stroke_ are triggered when user inputs the same character (ignore case) with the
38:   existing character when that character is valid for the applying diacritic.
39:   - `Aa` -> `Â`, `Ee` -> `Ê`, `Oo` -> `Ô`.
40:   - `Dd` -> `Đ`.
41: - _Breve_, _horn_ are triggered when user input `W`, `w` after the character when that character is
42:   valid for the applying diacritic. Otherwise, treat `W`, `w` literally.
43:   - `Aw` -> `Ă`.
44:   - `Ow` -> `Ơ`, `Uw` -> `Ư`.
45: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
46: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
47: 
48: #### Override rules
49: 
50: - New valid diacritic can override existing valid diacritic of the same base character.
51:   - `Ăa` -> `Â`, `Âw` -> `Ă`.
52:   - `Ôw` -> `Ơ`, `Ơo` -> `Ô`.
53: - Only `A`, `O` have override rules because they have more than one possible character diacritic.
54: - Keep tone untouched.
55: - The existing character keep its case, tone.
56: - Treat out of scope characters as literal.
57: 
58: #### Cancel rules
59: 
60: - The cancellation is triggered if and only if user inputs the character (ignore case) that would
61:   produce the existing diacritic for the same base character of the immediate previous character.
62: - The existing character keeps its case, tone, but the diacritic is removed.
63:   - Circumflex: `Âa` -> `Aa`, `Ốo` -> `Óo`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
64:   - Breve: `Ăw` -> `Aw`.
65:   - Horn: `Ưw` -> `Uw`... and so on for other cases of the same kind.
66:   - Stroke: `Đd` -> `Dd`, `đD` -> `dD`.
67: - The new character is literal, keeps its case, no tone.
68: - Switch to literal from the new character position and the following characters. Literal input
69:   remains until the new word.
70:   - Existing `Â`, input `aa`, result `Aaa`.
71: - When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again.
72: 
73: #### Auto-fill rules
74: 
75: - The auto-fill is only applied for `ươ` syllable immediately before the user input, when one of the
76:   character in the syllable is missing horn: `uơ` and `ưo`.
77: - Fill the missing _horn_ when the input is one of these character (ignore case): `C`, `I`, `M`,
78:   `N`, `P`, `T`, `U` and their lower cases.
79:   - Fill missing horn on `U`: `uơn` -> `ươn`.
80:   - Fill missing horn on `O`: `ưóp` -> `ướp`.
81: - The new input character is appended literally.
82: - Tone and case of existing characters are preserved on the original characters.
83: 
84: ### Tones
85: 
86: Tones let a word has many variants, each variant has different meaning. Only the following
87: Vietnamese vowel could have tones: `A`, `E`, `I`, `O`, `U`, `Y` and their corresponding variants.
88: These vowel could take any tone.
89: 
90: #### Level (ngang)
91: 
92: - The default tone, it's the vowel without any tone mark, e.g. `A`, `Ê`, `Ơ`...and all other
93:   corresponding variants.
94: - If a vowel has tone mark other than level, use `Z`, `z` to reset the tone to level.
95: 
96: #### Rising (sắc)
97: 
98: - Example of rising tone: `Á`, `Ế`, `Ớ`...and all other corresponding variants.
99: - Use `S`, `s` to put the rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `As` -> `Á`, `Ês` -> `Ế`, `Ơs`
100:   -> `Ớ`.
101: 
102: #### Falling (huyền)
103: 
104: - Example of falling tone: `À`, `Ề`, `Ờ`...and all other corresponding variants.
105: - Use `F`, `f` to put the falling tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Af` -> `À`, `Êf` -> `Ề`, `Ơf`
106:   -> `Ờ`.
107: 
108: #### Dipping-rising (hỏi)
109: 
110: - Example of dipping-rising tone: `Ả`, `Ể`, `Ở`...and all other corresponding variants.
111: - Use `R`, `r` to put the dipping-rising tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ar` -> `Ả`, `Êr` ->
112:   `Ể`, `Ơr` -> `Ở`.
113: 
114: #### Rising-glottalized (ngã)
115: 
116: - Example of rising-glottalized tone: `Ã`, `Ễ`, `Ỡ`...and all other corresponding variants.
117: - Use `X`, `x` to put the rising-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Ax` -> `Ã`, `Êx`
118:   -> `Ễ`, `Ơx` -> `Ỡ`.
119: 
120: #### Falling-glottalized (nặng)
121: 
122: - Example of falling-glottalized tone: `Ạ`, `Ệ`, `Ợ`...and all other corresponding variants.
123: - Use `J`, `j` to put the falling-glottalized tone to the Vietnamese vowels, e.g. `Aj` -> `Ạ`, `Êj`
124:   -> `Ệ`, `Ơj` -> `Ợ`.
125: 
126: #### Apply rules
127: 
128: - Only trigger apply tone when the buffer has existing characters. Otherwise, append the new
129:   character literally.
130: - Trigger apply tone when user inputs the trigger character for tones: `S`, `F`, `R`, `X`, `J` and
131:   their lower cases.
132: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
133: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels, append new character literally.
134: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and all of them don't have any tone (level), use _tone
135:   position rules_ to apply the tone for the word.
136: - If _tone position rules_ could not put tone to the pseudo-word, append new character literally.
137: 
138: #### Reset rules
139: 
140: - Only trigger reset tone when the buffer has existing character. Otherwise, append the new
141:   character literally.
142: - Trigger reset tone when user inputs the trigger character for reset: `Z`, `z`.
143: - _Scan pseudo-word_ to have the information for later steps.
144: - If the pseudo-word has at least one vowels and has tone (other than level) in one of the vowels,
145:   change that tone to level, keep case and diacritic.
146: - If the pseudo-word doesn't have any vowels or has at least one vowels and all of them don't have
147:   any tone (level), append the new character literally.
148: - If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal from the new character
149:   position and the following characters. Literal input remains until the new word.
150: 
151: #### Scan pseudo-word
152: 
153: - Scan pseudo-word is a process to get the information needed from the existing characters to put
154:   the tone in the correct position.
155: - The existing characters must not be empty.
156: - Iterate backward from the last existing character (the new input is the tone trigger character).
157: - If a vowel is found first, continue go backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no
158:   character to scan, then stop.
159: - If a consonant is found first, continue go backward until found the first vowel, then continue go
160:   backward until scanned the first consonant (singular) or no character to scan, then stop.
161: - After iterated, a context have been formed either (in the left to right order, not the reverse)
162:   `<consonants>`, `<vowels><consonants>`, `<consonant><vowels><consonants>`, `<vowels>` or
163:   `<consonant><vowels>`.
164: 
165: #### Tone position rules
166: 
167: The process of applying tone to a word go through many steps:
168: 
169: - Focus on the `<vowels>` in peusdo-word, it could be one vowel but could be multiple vowels, the
170:   tone will be applied on one of these vowels.
171: - Exceptions:
172:   - Word exactly `QU` (and their lower cases), could not put tone, this is a consonant only.
173: - If one vowel, put the tone on that vowel.
174: - If multiple vowels, follow these rules, priority from top down:
175:   - Vowels has `Ơ` (and its lower case), put on `Ơ`.
176:   - Vowels has one any of `Ê`, `Â`, `Ô`, `Ă`, `Ư` (and their lower cases), put on the listed vowels,
177:     prioritize the first to the last in the list.
178:   - Word start with `GI`, `QU` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels, put on next vowel, `I` /
179:     `U` count as part of the consonant.
180:   - Vowels exactly `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases), put on first vowel.
181:   - Vowels start with `OA`, `OE`, `OO`, `UY` (and their lower cases) and has more vowels or
182:     consonants, put on second vowel.
183:   - Otherwise, put on first vowel.
184: - Preserve case and diacritic on existing vowels.
185: - Could not put tone for other cases.

content.thinking: I'm checking the "Reset rules" section against the rest of the spec to identify any gaps or contradictions.

content.text:
Analysis of tone "Reset rules" (lines 138-149) in context of the whole spec:

## Gaps

1. **Missing literal-switch backspace recovery.** The diacritic Cancel rules (line 71) explicitly state: "When the user backspaces pass before the literal point, we switch to Vietnamese input again." Reset rules trigger an identical literal-switch (line 148-149) but say nothing about backspace recovery. Either it's the same behavior (then state it) or different (then specify).

2. **No `Z`/`z` cancel rule for level vowels.** Diacritics have a Cancel rule: typing the trigger of the existing diacritic removes it. The tone counterpart would be: typing `Z` after an already-level vowel cancels/literalizes. The spec instead routes that case into "append literally" (line 146-147), which is reasonable but the asymmetry with diacritic Cancel rules deserves to be called out, otherwise readers will wonder.

3. **Multiple toned vowels in pseudo-word — undefined.** Apply rules guarantee at most one tone per word ("all of them don't have any tone", line 134). But Reset rules say "has tone … in one of the vowels, change that tone to level" (line 144-145). It does not specify what to do if somehow more than one vowel carries a tone (e.g., from a previously literal-input state, or reading from buffer state produced outside Apply rules). Spec should either assert the invariant or define behavior.

4. **No example.** Every other subsection has at least one example. Reset rules has none. e.g., `Ár` no — `Áz` -> `A`, `Ưở` + `z` -> `Ưo`? Should be illustrated.

5. **Pseudo-word scope unclear.** Reset only acts on the pseudo-word found by Scan pseudo-word. If the toned vowel is outside that scan window (e.g., earlier in the buffer), no reset happens. This is implied but not stated. Compare with Apply rules which have the same constraint — both could share a clarifying note.

6. **Interaction with Auto-fill / Override / Cancel diacritic rules unspecified.** `Z` is not a trigger character for any diacritic action, so it should be safe, but the spec never declares that tone reset never affects diacritics (only tone). Line 145 says "keep case and diacritic" — good — but doesn't address whether `Z` could ever interact with diacritic state machines.

7. **`buffer_modification_index` / synthetic backspace contract.** Per AGENTS.md, every `State.add` action must decide `buffer_modification_index`. The Reset rules describe two outcomes (mutate the toned vowel in place vs. literal append) but don't tell the implementer where the modification starts in each case. Apply rules has the same omission, so perhaps out of scope for this spec, but worth flagging.

## Contradictions / friction

8. **"Buffer has existing character" wording (line 140) vs. "buffer has existing characters" in Apply rules (line 128).** Singular vs. plural. Minor, but Reset's singular form is technically more correct (one prior char is enough). Align them.

9. **Literal switch trigger condition is broader than necessary.** Line 148 says: "If the new character has been appended literally, switch to literal …". But line 146-147 lists two literal-append cases:
   - no vowels in pseudo-word
   - vowels exist but none has a tone

   In the *no vowels* case, there's nothing tone-related to "reset"; treating `z` as the start of a literal stretch of the word is consistent. In the *all-level vowels* case it is the same — but a user who simply mistyped `z` early gets locked into literal until next word. Diacritic Cancel literalizes only after a real cancellation; Reset literalizes after a *no-op*. This is an asymmetry worth confirming intentional.

10. **"Until the new word" undefined.** Both Cancel (line 69) and Reset (line 149) end literal mode "until the new word", but the spec never defines what starts a "new word" (whitespace? any non-letter? backspace past the literal point per line 71?). Needs a single canonical definition.

Which of these would you like to discuss / refine first?
